title: Red Wine on Linen
stainChemistry: Red wine anthocyanins hydrogen-bond to linen's cellulose hydroxyl groups — same mechanism as cotton, but linen's flax fibers are 30% stronger and more absorbent. Higher absorbency means wine wicks deeper into linen faster than cotton, but the cellulose chemistry is identical: pH 6.0–11.0, heat tolerance to 90°C. Linen wrinkles easily when wet and may yellow if bleached aggressively, but it handles the full tannin treatment sequence. The tannin polymers oxidize to brown cross-links with age and heat exposure.
whyThisWorks: Linen is a cellulose fiber with nearly identical chemistry to cotton — the same acid → oxidizer → acid neutralization sequence works. Cold flush first because tannin sets with heat. Acetic acid breaks the tannin-cellulose hydrogen bonds. Peroxide + ammonia oxidizes the anthocyanin chromophore. Vinegar neutralization is especially important on linen — the stain families database warns that 'linen may yellow if bleached aggressively.' The neutralization step prevents residual alkali from causing that yellowing. Linen's superior strength when wet means slightly more aggressive mechanical action is safe compared to cotton.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back of fabric, blot with white towel
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–70°F) — no hot water on tannin stains
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place linen face-down on clean white towel. Flush cold water from the back. Linen absorbs 20% of its weight in moisture — the wine has already wicked deep, so flushing from the back pushes the stain OUT rather than deeper. Blot firmly but don't scrub. Linen is strong wet, but rough handling causes permanent wrinkle lines.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (full strength on linen)
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with spotting brush, tamp gently, flush with water gun
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — steam at safe distance (6+ inches) only after tannin formula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Tannin-Go full strength — linen tolerates undiluted acetic acid formula. Tamp lightly with spotting brush. Linen's superior wet strength means you can use moderate mechanical action. The acid protonates tannin hydroxyl groups, disrupting their bond to cellulose. Flush with water gun at safe distance. Repeat if color persists.

spottingProtocol.technique: Controlled oxidation after complete tannin formula flush
spottingProtocol.temperature: Gentle steam activation acceptable on linen
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush tannin formula COMPLETELY first. Apply peroxide + ammonia (1 drop 28% ammonia per 8 oz peroxide). Steam at 6+ inches to activate. The HOO⁻ anion cleaves the anthocyanin's conjugated bond system. Linen handles this well — it tolerates hydrogen peroxide at A-grade (up to 6% concentration).

spottingProtocol.agent: Acetic acid neutralization (vinegar or dilute GAA)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Critical on linen: Dan Eisen specifies vinegar at 2 oz/gallon of rinse water. Linen is more prone to alkaline yellowing than cotton. Residual ammonia/alkaline chemistry continues attacking the cellulose and causes visible yellowing on white or light linen. Flush vinegar solution through the area, then rinse with cold water. Press while damp for best results — linen sets wrinkles when it dries.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately with clean white cloth. Flush with cold water. Apply white vinegar full strength, let sit 3–5 minutes, blot. Linen absorbs quickly — act fast, the first 5 minutes are critical.
Set stain: Soak in warm water (100°F) with OxiClean per label directions for 2–4 hours. Rinse and apply vinegar (2 oz per gallon) to prevent yellowing. Air dry and inspect. Repeat soak if needed.

When to stop: If two rounds of OxiClean soak + vinegar haven't removed a brown oxidized stain, take to a professional cleaner. Linen handles aggressive professional chemistry well, but the 'aggressive bleaching may yellow linen' warning means a pro should calibrate the bleach strength.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water on fresh wine stain — sets tannin permanently into cellulose
NEVER skip vinegar neutralization after peroxide/ammonia — linen yellows more readily than cotton from residual alkali
NEVER apply peroxide while tannin spotter is still wet — oxidizes tannin in place permanently
NEVER tumble dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets residual tannin
Handle wrinkles: press linen while damp — once dry, wrinkles set and require re-wetting to remove

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Full strength — primary tannin bond breaker on linen
professional.note: Safe undiluted on linen's robust cellulose structure

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.note: Linen rates A-grade for peroxide up to 6% concentration

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover
professional.note: Can follow with Street's General Spotter for complete wet-side treatment

professional.name: Glacial Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10)
professional.note: Essential for linen — prevents alkaline yellowing that linen is susceptible to

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.use: Extended soak for set wine on linen
consumer.note: Follow with vinegar rinse — this is non-negotiable on linen to prevent yellowing

consumer.name: Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
consumer.note: Reducing agent targets anthocyanin directly — good first response

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar
consumer.use: Primary tannin spotter + essential post-treatment neutralizer
consumer.note: Dual purpose on linen — breaks tannin bonds AND prevents the yellowing linen is known for

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: After vinegar treatment, apply and wait 3 minutes
consumer.note: Follow with vinegar rinse. Safe on linen at drugstore concentration.

escalation.when: Stain persists after OxiClean soak + peroxide treatment, or brown oxidized stain that won't respond to two treatment cycles
escalation.whatToTell: This is oxidized red wine on linen. I used cold water, acid tannin spotter, peroxide with ammonia, and vinegar neutralization. The tannin has polymerized past what consumer oxidizers can break. Linen can handle your strongest wet-side chemistry.
escalation.specialistType: Professional dry cleaner — linen handles all solvents and aggressive chemistry. PERC, hydrocarbon, GreenEarth all safe. They can use professional-strength oxidizers, oxalic acid for extreme cases, and concentrated tannin formulas.

solventNote: Pre-spot wet side with tannin formula before dry cleaning — tannin sets at machine temperature (140–150°F). Linen handles all solvents well — PERC, hydrocarbon, GreenEarth, K4 all rated excellent. Level with Sofspot before machine load. For set stains: Dan Eisen's professional escalation sequence applies — tannin formula → oxalic acid (test) → rust remover → peroxide + ammonia with steam. Always neutralize acid residue on cellulose fibers.
